pattern: On a strictly up-then-down sequence, comparing a midpoint to its right neighbor tells you which side of the summit you are on, so binary search finds the peak in logarithmic time.
hints: The single peak is the only index whose value is greater than both of its neighbors, and every step before it rises while every step after it falls.
Comparing the middle element with the one just to its right tells you whether the peak is to the right (still rising) or at or to the left (already falling).
Binary search on the index: if arr[mid] < arr[mid + 1] move the low bound past mid, otherwise move the high bound to mid; the bounds converge on the peak.

edge_inputs.description: shortest possible mountain, peak in the middle
steep single-step descent after the peak
peak sits at the very last possible index for a mountain
peak sits at the very first possible index
long gentle slope on both sides

stress.description: large mountain built by choosing a random peak position then strictly increasing up to it and strictly decreasing after
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, size)
    peak = rng.randrange(1, n - 1)
    arr = [0] * n
    cur = rng.randrange(0, 5)
    arr[0] = cur
    for i in range(1, peak + 1):
        cur += rng.randrange(1, 4)
        arr[i] = cur
    for i in range(peak + 1, n):
        cur -= rng.randrange(1, 4)
        if cur < 0:
            cur = 0
        arr[i] = cur
    return [arr]
stress.note: strictly bumpy by construction so the peak index is unique
